nodes.name: Makandure Madush
nodes.source_file: dbsjeyaraj.com — 'Downfall in Dubai of Sri Lanka's Most Wanted Drug Dealer Makandure Madush'
nodes.source_excerpt: Drug kingpin; ran a Dubai-based narcotics network. Arrested in Dubai 4 Feb 2019, returned to Sri Lanka 5 May 2019 under the PTA, shot dead in police custody 20 Oct 2020.

nodes.name: Kanjipani Imran
nodes.source_file: Daily Mirror — 'Kanjipani Imran, three others deported from Dubai'
nodes.source_excerpt: Colombo underworld figure; arrested with Madush in Dubai 4 Feb 2019 and deported to Sri Lanka.

nodes.name: Angoda Lokka
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Drug lord; reportedly died of a heart attack in Coimbatore, India in 2020 while directing operations from abroad.

nodes.name: Harak Kata
nodes.source_file: News First — 'Underworld gangster Harak Kata extradited to Sri Lanka from Madagascar'
nodes.source_excerpt: Heroin trafficker with an international network (Dubai, Malaysia, Maldives, Seychelles, Madagascar); arrested in Madagascar and extradited to Sri Lanka 15 Mar 2023; held under the PTA.

nodes.name: Wele Suda
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Major heroin trafficker; arrested in Pakistan (Karachi) in Feb 2015 and extradited; continued to run operations from prison. Serving a life sentence.

nodes.name: Kalu Sagara
nodes.source_file: Daily Mirror — 'Who was behind the arrest of Makandure Madush?'
nodes.source_excerpt: One-time associate of Madush who reportedly tipped off Dubai Police and the Sri Lankan STF to his location.

nodes.name: Ranale Samayan
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Underworld gang leader; killed in 2017, reportedly by the rival gang of Angoda Lokka and Makandure Madush.

nodes.name: Kudu Salindu
nodes.source_file: News First — 'Underworld gangster Harak Kata extradited to Sri Lanka from Madagascar'
nodes.source_excerpt: Organized-crime suspect arrested in Madagascar alongside Harak Kata and brought to Sri Lanka in March 2023.

nodes.name: Ganemulla Sanjeewa
nodes.source_file: Lanka News Web — 'Mobile phone and accessories found in cells of notorious criminals at Boossa Prison'
nodes.source_excerpt: Organized-crime and drug-trafficking convict; a smartphone and accessories were found in his Boossa Prison cell.

nodes.name: Kosgoda Sujee
nodes.source_file: dbsjeyaraj.com — 'Downfall in Dubai of Sri Lanka's Most Wanted Drug Dealer Makandure Madush'
nodes.source_excerpt: Named among the group that helped Madush establish himself in Dubai before falling out with him.

nodes.name: Kirulapone Merrill
nodes.source_file: dbsjeyaraj.com — 'Downfall in Dubai of Sri Lanka's Most Wanted Drug Dealer Makandure Madush'
nodes.source_excerpt: Named among the group that helped Madush establish himself in Dubai before falling out with him.

nodes.name: Siddique
nodes.source_file: dbsjeyaraj.com — 'Downfall in Dubai of Sri Lanka's Most Wanted Drug Dealer Makandure Madush'
nodes.source_excerpt: Named among the group that helped Madush establish himself in Dubai before falling out with him.

nodes.name: Soththi Upali
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Underworld figure active 1970–1998; allied with political patrons of the era. Killed 17 Dec 1998.

nodes.name: Chinthaka Amarasinghe
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Underworld figure who reportedly sought to avenge his father's killing by Soththi Upali's faction; killed 1996.

nodes.name: Dhammika Amarasinghe
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Brother of Chinthaka Amarasinghe; active 1990s–2004. Killed inside Colombo Magistrate's Court in Jan 2004.

nodes.name: Kalu Ajith
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Gang leader active 1980s–1997; his gang was blamed for the 1996 killing of Chinthaka Amarasinghe. Killed 16 Jul 1997.

nodes.name: Nawala Nihal
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Described as a godfather of the Colombo underworld; blamed for the 1997 killing of Kalu Ajith. Died 2006.

nodes.name: Kaduwela Wasantha
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Underworld figure reportedly allied with Chinthaka Amarasinghe and Kalu Ajith. Killed 26 May 2002.

nodes.name: Moratu Saman
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Underworld figure once allied with Dhammika Amarasinghe before a violent falling-out. Died 2003.

nodes.name: Olcott
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Heroin trafficker linked to a failed 1999 LTTE assassination attempt on President Chandrika Kumaratunga; lived in exile in Tamil Nadu. Killed in an STF raid in Sept 2010.

nodes.name: Kimbula-Ela Guna
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Major drug dealer with LTTE links; involved in the failed 1999 assassination attempt on President Kumaratunga; arrested in Tamil Nadu in 2008.

nodes.name: Thel Bala
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
nodes.source_excerpt: Jaffna-region drug dealer linked to the failed 1999 assassination attempt on President Kumaratunga. Died 2017.

nodes.name: Zahran Hashim
nodes.source_file: Jamestown Foundation — 'The Mastermind of the Sri Lankan Easter Sunday Attacks: Zahran Hashim'
nodes.source_excerpt: Founder of the National Thowheeth Jama'ath and suspected ringleader of the 21 Apr 2019 Easter attacks; suicide bomber at the Shangri-La Hotel.

nodes.name: Rilwan Hashim
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Brother of Zahran Hashim; reportedly helped build explosives. Killed in the Sainthamaruthu raid on 26 Apr 2019.

nodes.name: Zainee Hashim
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Brother of Zahran Hashim; named among those directly linked to the attack network.

nodes.name: Naufer Moulavi
nodes.source_file: Ada Derana — 'Zahran planned to launch Easter attack using 20 bombers, PCoI hears'
nodes.source_excerpt: Named by the Minister of Public Security, after the PCoI report, as the mastermind of the Easter attacks; in custody.

nodes.name: Inshaf Ahmed
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suicide bomber at the Cinnamon Grand; owned the Colossus Copper factory reportedly used to fabricate suicide vests.

nodes.name: Ilham Ahmed
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suicide bomber at the Shangri-La Hotel; younger brother of Inshaf Ahmed.

nodes.name: Abdul Latheef Jameel
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suicide bomber (Tropical Inn, Dehiwala); had been investigated in 2014 by an Australian Joint Counter Terrorism Team over reported ISIS links via operative Neil Prakash.

nodes.name: Mohamed Azam
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suicide bomber at the Kingsbury Hotel; NTJ member.

nodes.name: Mohamed Nassar
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suicide bomber at Zion Church, Batticaloa; NTJ member.

nodes.name: Hasthun
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suicide bomber at St. Sebastian's Church, Negombo; husband of Pulasthini Rajendran.

nodes.name: Alawdeen Ahmed Muad
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suicide bomber at St. Anthony's Shrine, Colombo; NTJ member.

nodes.name: Ahmed Milhan
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Suspected NTJ figure, reportedly deported from the Middle East and expected to be a successor leader.

nodes.name: Army Mohideen
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Reportedly provided military-style training to the attackers.

nodes.name: Fathima Ilham
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Wife of Ilham Ahmed; detonated explosives during the Dematagoda raid on 26 Apr 2019, killing herself, her children and three police officers.

nodes.name: Pulasthini Rajendran
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Wife of the bomber Hasthun; killed in the Sainthamaruthu raid on 26 Apr 2019.

nodes.name: Abu Hind
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: A contact in India with whom Zahran reportedly maintained regular phone communication from 2018.

nodes.name: National Thowheeth Jamaath
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Local militant Islamist group founded by Zahran Hashim; designated a terrorist organisation on 27 Apr 2019.

nodes.name: Jammiyathul Millathu Ibrahim
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Jammiyathul Millathu Ibrahim — a splinter group designated a terrorist organisation alongside the NTJ on 27 Apr 2019.

nodes.name: Islamic State
aliases: Islamic State of Iraq and Syria
nodes.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
nodes.source_excerpt: Islamic State; its Amaq agency claimed the attacks, but the CID found no evidence of direct IS operational involvement.

edges.source_file: Ada Derana — 'Makandure Madush arrested in Dubai'
edges.source_excerpt: Madush was arrested in Dubai on 4 Feb 2019; Kanjipani Imran was among those arrested with him and later deported.

edges.source_file: dbsjeyaraj.com — 'Downfall in Dubai of Sri Lanka's Most Wanted Drug Dealer Makandure Madush'
edges.source_excerpt: Imran had partnered with Makandure Madush to build a syndicate controlling maritime trafficking routes through the Indian Ocean.

edges.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
edges.source_excerpt: Ranale Samayan was killed in 2017 by the rival gang of Angoda Lokka and Makandure Madush — indicating an operational alliance between them.

edges.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
edges.source_excerpt: Samayan's death (2017) is attributed to the rival gang of Angoda Lokka and Makandure Madush.

edges.source_file: Times of Addu — 'Sri Lankan drug trafficker Harak Kata with links to Maldives arrested in Dubai'
edges.source_excerpt: Harak Kata is described as a close associate of the slain underworld don 'Angoda Lokka'.

edges.source_file: News First — 'Underworld gangster Harak Kata extradited to Sri Lanka from Madagascar'
edges.source_excerpt: Harak Kata and Kudu Salindu were arrested in Madagascar and brought to Sri Lanka in March 2023.

edges.source_file: Daily Mirror — 'Who was behind the arrest of Makandure Madush?'
edges.source_excerpt: Kalu Sagara was a one-time friend of Madush; drug-trade rivalry turned them against each other.

edges.source_excerpt: Kalu Sagara reportedly tipped off Dubai Police and the Sri Lankan STF about Madush's location, leading to his arrest.

edges.source_excerpt: Wele Suda, Kosgoda Sujee, Kirulapone Merrill and Siddique had helped Madush set up in Dubai.

edges.source_file: dbsjeyaraj.com — 'Downfall in Dubai of Sri Lanka's Most Wanted Drug Dealer Makandure Madush'

edges.source_excerpt: Wele Suda, Kosgoda Sujee, Kirulapone Merrill and Siddique had helped Madush set up in Dubai.

edges.source_file: dbsjeyaraj.com — 'Downfall in Dubai of Sri Lanka's Most Wanted Drug Dealer Makandure Madush'
edges.source_excerpt: Wele Suda, Kosgoda Sujee, Kirulapone Merrill and Siddique had helped Madush set up in Dubai.

edges.source_excerpt: The same group, feeling sidelined by Madush, was reportedly involved in setting him up for arrest.

edges.source_excerpt: The same group, feeling sidelined by Madush, was reportedly involved in setting him up for arrest.

edges.source_file: Lanka News Web — 'Mobile phone and accessories found in cells of notorious criminals at Boossa Prison'
edges.source_excerpt: An STF sweep recovered a smartphone and accessories concealed in the cells of two notorious inmates, 'Ganemulla Sanjeewa' and 'Wele Suda', at Boossa high-security prison.

edges.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters
edges.source_excerpt: Chinthaka Amarasinghe was reportedly motivated by avenging his father's death at the hands of Soththi Upali's faction.

edges.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters

edges.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters

edges.source_file: Wikipedia — List of Sri Lankan mobsters

edges.source_excerpt: Muhammed Naufer, named as the mastermind, was a leading figure of the NTJ network.

edges.source_file: Wikipedia — 2019 Sri Lanka Easter bombings
edges.source_excerpt: Jammiyathul Millathu Ibrahim (JMI) was a splinter group designated a terrorist organisation alongside the NTJ.

meta.title: Sri Lanka Illicit Networks — Temporal Multiplex Graph
meta.disclaimer: Analytical model compiled ONLY from public reporting (Wikipedia, PCoI reporting, named Sri Lankan news outlets). Not a determination of guilt. Confidence tags encode source strength: EXTRACTED = stated plainly in an official record or by named reporting; INFERRED = probable but not adjudicated; AMBIGUOUS = alleged / contested. The three networks are not linked in the public record.

layers.FINANCIAL: Money flows and shared illicit enterprise (drug operations, funding, enterprise violence)
layers.PRISON_CO_LOCATION: Co-located in a prison / remand facility
layers.TRANSNATIONAL: Cross-border links: foreign networks, smuggling routes, overseas handlers

confidence.EXTRACTED: 1.0 — stated plainly in an official record or by named reporting
confidence.INFERRED: 0.7 — probable link reporting supports but has not adjudicated
